title: Chapter 22: The One Who Lights the Lamp
paragraphs: Lord Valthor's ice spears tore through the monsters' flesh with mathematical, merciless precision. The legion of the dead, knowing neither fatigue nor fear, formed an impenetrable wall of shields made of old, rusted bone. They held the flanks, giving the refugees a chance to breathe. Dzhanavar and Rene fought in the center, their movements merging into a single, desperate rhythm of survival.
It seemed this absurd union of outcasts had managed to do the impossible—they had stopped the flow at the very gates of Etcha.
But the rotting skies did not know how to lose.

A muffled, visceral crack rolled across the firmament, from which the city's marble walls were covered with deep fissures. The rift vortex, which had previously spewed monsters one by one, suddenly expanded threefold, pouring out concentrated darkness.

From the torn fabric of the universe, an Abomination slowly crawled out, its bony growths clawing at the edges of reality. A creature the size of a fortress tower, whose body consisted of intertwined gray limbs, spikes, and pulsating ulcers.

As the Abomination landed heavily on the ashen tract, the earth shook so violently that half of Valthor's skeletons simply crumbled to dust. The shockwave threw Rene and Dzhanavar back dozens of paces. Kirah, who tried to block the monster's path, was swept away with one careless sweep of a colossal paw. The huge demon crashed into the crowd of refugees, choking on his own blood.
The Abomination towered over the people huddled at the gates. It slowly raised its thousand-ton, blade-studded limb, preparing to crush them into dust with one blow.

Lord Valthor raised his hand, sending a barrage of ice blades at the monster, but the vampire's ancient magic simply evaporated before reaching the gray armor. The Abomination was too large. Too absolute.
Yashil, clutching the bent iron pipe, stood directly under the descending shadow of death. He did not look away. He had stood for these people for seven years; he wasn't going to stop now.
But at that moment, the space between the goblin and the monster tore with a piercing, ringing whistle.
From above, directly from the jade parapet of Etcha, a black meteor fell.
Avotu.

The last apprentice of Sivarus landed with frightening grace. The Blade of Nature in his hand did not emit a chaotic howl as before. Now the sword sang. Purely and lethally. The black flame above the elf's pale face burned steadily—still dark, still cold, but no longer consuming itself.
Avotu did not wait for the Abomination's strike. He became the strike himself.

The elf disappeared in a flash of black fire and appeared directly above the monster's colossal paw. One flawless stroke of the sword—and a condensed gale of air severed the giant limb with a wet crunch. The Abomination let out a deafening screech, recoiling. Acidic blood cascaded onto the stones.
Avotu landed in front of Yashil, shielding the goblin and the crowd of refugees with his back.
"You promised to be a shield, traveler," Yashil spoke softly through the roar of battle, a faint smile flickering on his bloody lips.
"And I will keep the oath, goblin," Avotu replied without turning around.

The Behemoth of the Void, roaring in pain, collapsed all its unimaginable power upon the elf. Avotu moved at the limit of his physical and magical capabilities. He parried blows capable of crushing mountains, deflected them with sliding blocks, and dealt compensatory, precise wounds.
But the Abomination felt no fatigue. Radiation ash clogged Avotu's lungs, his muscles burning after days of wandering and spiritual agony. With each parry, the Blade of Nature descended more slowly.

The monster sensed weakness. The Abomination stepped back, its giant ribcage expanding, drawing in the surrounding air and remnants of mana. The creature prepared to unleash a wave of pure, concentrated entropy—an attack that did not just kill the body, but erased the very essence of matter.
Avotu realized this. He knew that he could not dodge—behind him stood hundreds of defenseless lives, Lord Valthor, Kirah, and Yashil.

The great elf drove the Blade of Nature deep into the stone slabs of the tract, knelt on one knee, and raised both hands, concentrating all the remnants of his life force to create an absolute barrier. He prepared to die so that they might live.
But time in fading worlds flows differently. When the fabric of reality disintegrates, seconds stretch into a thick, transparent resin.
Yashil saw the Abomination open its maw. He saw Avotu's shoulders hunch under the invisible weight.

In the small goblin's head, there was neither fear nor doubt. He had no magic to stop this blow. He had no armor to survive it. He had only his life—a light, tiny green life that this world had always considered worthless dirt.
Yashil threw down his rusty pipe.
He rushed forward. His bare feet slid silently across the acid- and ash-covered stone. He ran past the horrified Dzhanavar, past Rene who had lowered her blades.
Avotu, concentrated on creating the shield, did not notice the tiny figure.
The goblin's small, calloused hands pushed the armor-clad elf in the chest with incredible, desperate strength impossible for his body.
The blow was unexpected. Avotu lost his balance and fell onto his back. The barrier shattered with a quiet chime before it could even form.
The elf threw back his head, and his eyes widened in primal horror.

Where he had stood a second ago, the figure of Yashil now towered. The goblin did not cover himself with his hands. He stood straight, squaring his narrow shoulders, and looked directly into the maw of the oncoming death.
"To give is not the lot of fools," Yashil whispered with barely moving lips.

A wave of black, concentrated entropy burst from the Abomination's maw and collapsed with a deafening roar upon the spot where the goblin stood. The stone of the Great Tract instantly turned to dust, forming a deep, smoking crater.
The shockwave scattered everyone nearby.
When the blinding flash faded, a dead, vacuum silence hung over the battlefield. It was scarier than any screams.

Avotu, coughing blood, struggled to rise on his elbows. The Behemoth of the Void, having exhausted its energy supply on this colossal blow, sat heavily, slowly crumbling into pieces—its body could not withstand its own power.
But the elf did not look at the defeated enemy.
He looked at the edge of the smoking crater. There, on the charred stones, lay a small, motionless green body.
A sound came from under the rubble. It was not a battle cry. It was not the roar of a beast. It was the sound of a soul being torn to pieces.

Kirah, heavily dragging a wounded paw, crawled out from under the debris. The huge demon saw the green blood mixed with gray ash and fell to his knees. His burning ruby eyes were flooded with an impossible, stinging moisture.
The wind over Etcha died down. The leaden skies froze, as if reality itself held its breath.
